There is a view=C2=A0that crypto was a low-interest-rates phenomenon, but s=
tablecoins are=C2=A0really=C2=A0a low-interest-rates phenomenon. The idea o=
f the most popular (=E2=80=9Cfully backed,=E2=80=9D non-algorithmic) stable=
coins is:
=09You give some company $1.=09It promises to give you back the $1 when you=
 want it.
  [1]

=09That promise=C2=A0lives on the blockchain;=C2=A0it can be traded on the =
blockchain, and it represents $1 on the blockchain.

This description is very, very close to the standard story of=C2=A0banking:=
=C2=A0You put one paper dollar in the bank, you get back a receipt saying y=
ou=E2=80=99ve got $1 in the bank, and you can use that receipt for $1 in th=
e bank to transact with because it=C2=A0is a dollar. Your money in your ban=
k account is not a=C2=A0claim=C2=A0on money, not an IOU for money; your mon=
ey in your bank account=C2=A0is money. A stablecoin=C2=A0is money, in the s=
pecific context of some blockchain.
However, two other things that bank accounts have are:
=09Branches, tellers, customer service, compliance,=C2=A0stuff like that; a=
nd=09
Interest=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0not all of the time, and not so much over the =
past decade, but in an environment of high interest rates it becomes reason=
able to expect to get paid interest on at least some of the money you keep =
in the bank.

Stablecoins can be like three guys in an undisclosed location who provide g=
ood customer service to a dozen big counterparties and have an inscrutable =
website for everyone else. And they tend not to pay interest.
This was a minor omission when banks also tended not to pay interest, but a=
s rates have gone up, stablecoins seem like an incredibly good business to =
be in? You have a giant pile of money, you can park it somewhere quite safe=
 and earn like 5% interest, you pay $0 of that interest to your depositors,=
 you pay not very much more than $0 of that interest to your compliance dep=
artment, you do not have much in the way of operating costs, and you collec=
t 5% of a giant pile of money basically for having come up with this idea a=
 few years ago. Of course the downside is that if crypto was a low-interest=
-rates phenomenon eventually people might=C2=A0lose interest in crypto and =
the demand for stablecoins would dry up. But  Tether is fine I guess?

Crypto company Tether pledged to buy more bitcoin for its stablecoin reserv=
es, adding to the $1.5 billion of bitcoin already backing its dollar-pegged=
 token.
The company said starting this month it planned to use up to 15% of its net=
 operating profits to purchase bitcoin. It didn=E2=80=99t indicate how long=
 or consistently it planned to make the purchases. Rising interest rates bo=
osted Tether=E2=80=99s profits to $1.48 billion in the first quarter. =E2=
=80=A6
Tether said it plans for its bitcoin investments to be part of the company=
=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cexcess reserves,=E2=80=9D which is what it calls additi=
onal reserves beyond the value of its liabilities=E2=80=94the market cap of=
 the tether stablecoin. On May 16, Tether=E2=80=99s excess reserves amounte=
d to over $2.4 billion, according to its website. =E2=80=A6
Last week the stablecoin issuer reported that it held more than $69 billion=
 in cash and cash equivalents at the end of March, including $53 billion in=
vested in U.S. Treasury bills, $7.5 billion in overnight reverse-repurchase=
 agreements and $7.5 billion in money-market funds. The crypto company had =
billions of dollars in precious metals, secured loans and other investments=
 that include digital tokens.

Tether=E2=80=99s latest  reserves report shows  roughly $79.4 billion of Te=
ther stablecoins outstanding, backed by =E2=80=9Cat least=E2=80=9D $81.8 bi=
llion of assets, for an excess of $2.4 billion, or, roughly speaking, a cap=
ital ratio of about 3%. When we   talked about Tether a year ago, that rati=
o was 0.2%. A 3% capital ratio for a bank with mostly very safe short-term =
assets like Treasury bills would be pretty normal
  [2]
; a 0.2% capital ratio for any sort of=C2=A0bank=C2=A0at all is hair-raisin=
g. Tether has gone from alarmingly thinly capitalized to reasonably well ca=
pitalized over the past year, because it is a pile of money invested in sho=
rt-term money-market instruments and rates have gone up a lot. Now Tether e=
arns a lot of interest =E2=80=94 it earned more than half of its excess res=
erves=C2=A0last quarter=C2=A0=E2=80=94 and it puts that interest in the ban=
k, and that means it has more than enough money to pay back all the Tethers=
 out there.
Well, no, I mean, it doesn=E2=80=99t put the interest in the=C2=A0bank; it =
puts it in=C2=A0Bitcoin. (Or it puts 15% of it in Bitcoin.) Still, currentl=
y,=C2=A0that is all house money:
=E2=80=9CEvery single token in the market is and would remain fully backed =
even if the bitcoin price were to go down to zero tomorrow,=E2=80=9D [Tethe=
r Chief Technology Officer Paolo]=C2=A0Ardoino said in an email. =E2=80=9CT=
ether could distribute the entire amount invested in bitcoin to its shareho=
lders, and the peg to USD will not be affected. In such a scenario, Tether =
would still have $1B of excess reserves.=E2=80=9D
The ideal way to run Tether, for the people running Tether, would be:
=09Have like $80 billion of Tethers outstanding.=09Put $80 billion into the=
 safest possible stuff, short-term risk-free instruments, Treasury bills an=
d reverse repos with strong counterparties collateralized by Treasuries.=09=
Earn like $4 billion of interest on that stuff??=09Put $1 billion of the in=
terest into more safe stuff, just to be safe.=09Put $1 billion of the inter=
est into crazy stuff, to try to grow your assets and make more profits.=09P=
ay yourself $2 billion of bonuses, you=E2=80=99ve earned it.

People are very suspicious of Tether, for basically good reasons:
=09It does a long-running comedy routine where it constantly promises to pr=
ovide audited financial statements any day now, and never does.
  [3]

=09It has a history of saying that each Tether is fully backed by safe US d=
ollar assets, while in fact some of them were sometimes backed by   dodgy l=
oans to Tether=E2=80=99s affiliates.=09It seems clear that some Tethers hav=
e been created,=C2=A0not=C2=A0by customers depositing $1 and getting back o=
ne Tether, but by customers depositing $2 worth of Bitcoin (or less?) and  =
 getting back one Tether as a loan. This is sort of fractional reserve bank=
ing for crypto, and it=C2=A0means that if the price of Bitcoin drops and th=
e borrower can=E2=80=99t pay back the loan, Tether could be partially unbac=
ked.

The counterargument is that this is such a good and easy business it would =
be silly=C2=A0for Tether to take any risk: If you can earn more than a bill=
ion dollars of interest=C2=A0per quarter=C2=A0on safe assets with no real e=
xpenses, why do anything else?=C2=A0
I suppose part of the answer is that, if you run Tether, you do not want th=
is business to go away. If you are the bank of crypto, the main risks to yo=
ur business are:
=09People do not trust your reserves, so they take their money out, or=09Pe=
ople do not trust=C2=A0crypto, so they don=E2=80=99t need any Tethers, so t=
hey take their money out.

If you=E2=80=99ve got some spare cash to buy Bitcoin and support the system=
, that might be a good use of it.

   =20
      Is blitzscaling illegal?
   =20
 =20

Here is a pretty standard story that people used to tell about Uber Technol=
ogies Inc., back when it was a tech unicorn,=C2=A0before it went public:
=09Uber sells you a nicer car ride than a taxi, at a lower price.=09How doe=
s it manage this? Well, by losing money on every ride.=09How does it manage=
=C2=A0that? Well, it raises billions of dollars from venture capitalists, w=
hich it uses to subsidize=C2=A0the losses.=C2=A0=09How does it manage=C2=A0=
that? Well, it tells the venture capitalists a good story. In the story it =
tells the VCs, Uber grows its market share rapidly by offering the best pro=
duct at the lowest price. It builds network effects: Every rider=C2=A0wants=
 to use Uber because it has the best deals, which means every driver wants =
to drive for Uber because it has all the passengers, which means that every=
 rider=C2=A0really=C2=A0wants to use Uber because all the drivers are there=
, etc. The network effects make this something of a winner-take-all busines=
s, and if Uber wins then it will get all the drivers and all the passengers=
; it will be the app that people use whenever they need a ride. They will s=
top hailing cabs on the street, or telephoning car services. They will also=
 stop taking buses or subways or personal cars; Uber will become just the w=
ay to travel. Bus routes will close, subways will wither, cab drivers will =
become Uber drivers. Uber=E2=80=99s total addressable market is large, and =
its market share will grow. Venture capitalists love growing market shares =
and large total addressable markets.=09And, look, when Uber is 99% of the t=
ransportation market, it probably won=E2=80=99t lose money on every ride? Y=
ou can wave at reasons =E2=80=94=C2=A0efficiency? monetizing user data? sel=
f-driving cars? =E2=80=94=C2=A0but one obvious reason is=C2=A0it can raise =
prices. If the taxis are gone and the subways have withered, what are peopl=
e gonna do? They=E2=80=99re gonna pay more for Uber.

Again, this is just a common story that people told about Uber. I don=E2=80=
=99t think it was true in every particular at the time, and it did not exac=
tly work out that way. But it was kind of true, and it kind of worked out t=
hat way. Uber is  not exactly profitable, but it is profitable on an  adjus=
ted Ebitda basis. And prices have gone up. Henry Grabar  wrote last year:

It=E2=80=99s the end of a decade in which we changed our systems, our habit=
s, even our architecture, around the assumption that we could be driven aro=
und for cheap.
The cynical assumption was always that Uber was burning all that investor c=
ash in order to corner the market. Once it killed off car service, taxi car=
tels, and its ride-hail rivals, the company would stop charging riders less=
 than it was paying drivers and prices would have to go up. On Monday morni=
ng, an Uber from Manhattan to JFK Airport was $100=E2=80=94nearly double th=
e fixed yellow cab rate. But good luck finding a yellow cab!

One name for this story=C2=A0was =E2=80=9Cblitzscaling,=E2=80=9D and we  ta=
lked about it a  lot; this idea that startups should try to win in winner-t=
ake-all markets by using buckets of VC money to scale up as quickly as poss=
ible was pretty popular. Another  name for this  story was =E2=80=9Cthe Mov=
iepass economy,=E2=80=9D this idea that all those buckets of VC money, brie=
fly, subsidized normal people=E2=80=99s lifestyles: You could get cheap car=
 rides and food delivery and movie tickets, because VCs were paying for it,=
 because VCs loved user growth more than anything, because they thought tha=
t with enough users any company could flip into being profitable.
But another name for this story might be=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cpredatory pricing=E2=
=80=9D? Loosely speaking,  in US antitrust law,=C2=A0it is illegal for a fi=
rm to price its goods below cost with the goal of driving out competitors s=
o it can raise prices to monopoly levels. This is an  infamously squishy th=
eory, since after all =E2=80=9Cprice reductions are the hallmark of competi=
tion, and the tangible benefit that consumers perhaps most desire from the =
economic system=E2=80=9D: It=E2=80=99s kinda weird for companies to get in=
=C2=A0trouble with the antitrust authorities for=C2=A0cutting=C2=A0prices. =
It=E2=80=99s not like companies go around=C2=A0saying =E2=80=9Cwe are cutti=
ng prices to below our marginal cost in order to drive out competitors and =
get a monopoly.=E2=80=9D
But they came pretty close in the blitzscaling boom. Here is a fun paper ti=
tled =E2=80=9CVenture Predation,=E2=80=9D by Matthew Wansley and Samuel Wei=
nstein:

Predatory pricing is a strategy firms use to suppress competition. The pred=
ator prices below its own costs to force its rivals out of the market. Afte=
r they exit, the predator raises its prices to supracompetitive levels and =
recoups the cost of predation. The Supreme Court has described predatory pr=
icing as =E2=80=9Crarely tried=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Crarely successful=E2=
=80=9D and has established a liability standard that is nearly impossible f=
or plaintiffs to satisfy. We argue that one kind of company thinks predator=
y pricing is worth trying and at least potentially successful=E2=80=94ventu=
re-backed startups.
A venture predator is a startup that uses venture finance to price below it=
s costs, chase its rivals out of the market, and grab market share. Venture=
 capitalists (VCs) are motivated to fund predation=E2=80=94and startup foun=
ders are motivated to execute it=E2=80=94because it can fuel rapid, exponen=
tial growth. Critically, for VCs and founders, a predator does not need to =
recoup its losses for the strategy to succeed. The VCs and founders just ne=
ed to create the impression that recoupment is possible, so they can sell t=
heir shares at an attractive price to later investors who anticipate years =
of monopoly pricing. In this Article, we argue that venture predation can h=
arm consumers, distort market incentives, and misallocate capital away from=
 genuine innovations. We consider reforms to antitrust law and securities r=
egulation to deter it.

If you were running the third-biggest ride-hailing startup =E2=80=94=C2=A0o=
r if you drove a taxi? =E2=80=94=C2=A0and you were undercut by Uber=E2=80=
=99s venture-subsidized prices, maybe you should sue?

 =20
   =20
      FDIC HTM
   =20
 =20

We   talked yesterday about a proposal to let banks pay the US Federal Depo=
sit Insurance Corp. special assessment using Treasury bonds. The idea is th=
at, as interest rates went up, Treasury prices fell; banks that owned a lot=
 of Treasuries (or US agency mortgage bonds) had large mark-to-market losse=
s. This sparked bank runs, which caused some banks to fail and forced the F=
DIC to bail out depositors. Because of the large losses on the banks=E2=80=
=99 bond portfolios, the FDIC=E2=80=99s insurance fund was out of pocket bi=
llions of dollars. The FDIC replenishes this fund by assessing banks for th=
e money, and it has proposed to do so. But some of the banks are still hurt=
ing; they don=E2=80=99t want to pay billions of dollars to the FDIC right n=
ow, and they still have these lurking mark-to-market losses on their Treasu=
ry portfolios. So they proposed to pay the assessment=C2=A0in Treasuries:=
=C2=A0If a bank owes the FDIC $100, it could give the FDIC Treasury bonds w=
ith a face amount of=C2=A0$100, which are now worth $90 or whatever. The ba=
nk saves some money and gets some surprisingly toxic assets off its balance=
 sheet.
Then of course the FDIC gets underpaid, but the point of the plan is that i=
f the FDIC just holds the bonds to maturity, it gets the full $100. The und=
erlying idea here is that these bonds are =E2=80=9Cworth=E2=80=9D $100 in s=
ome abstract sense, they are worth $90 at market values, banks carried them=
 on their balance sheets at $100 on the assumption that they could hold the=
m to maturity and get back $100, and then runs on bank deposits called that=
 assumption into question: If you have to sell the bonds today to pay back =
depositors, they are only worth $90. But if you transfer the bonds to the F=
DIC, which is=C2=A0not=C2=A0funded by demand deposits,=C2=A0it=C2=A0can hol=
d the bonds to maturity; it is the US government. Moving the bonds from a b=
ank where they=E2=80=99re worth $90 to a government agency where they=E2=80=
=99re worth $100 just creates value. The Wall Street Journal  summarized=C2=
=A0this argument:
Supporters say the government would hold the securities until maturity, all=
owing them to recover principal and interest on the debt. The government wo=
uld suffer no losses, they say.
This argument is not at all=C2=A0right=C2=A0as a matter of the time value o=
f money, but it has a certain crude accounting appeal; it is not=C2=A0so=C2=
=A0far off the argument for   the Federal Reserve's Bank Term Funding Progr=
am,=C2=A0which will=C2=A0lend banks=C2=A0$100 against these bonds. But  the=
re is another objection to it, which is: Who says that the FDIC can hold th=
ese bonds to maturity? What if the FDIC depletes its insurance fund doing b=
ailouts, replenishes it by getting a bunch of long-dated Treasury bonds fro=
m banks, and then has to do more bailouts? Where will it get the money from=
? The insurance fund is full of long-dated Treasuries: Presumably it has to=
 sell them?
  [4]

The point is that the FDIC fund is not exactly=C2=A0runnable, but it is sor=
t of second-order runnable: If there is a run on rickety banks, that will l=
ead to a run on the FDIC=E2=80=99s insurance fund, as it will be needed to =
pay off those banks=E2=80=99 depositors. (This makes the FDIC a little bit =
  like Silvergate Capital Corp., a regular bank that provided banking servi=
ces to crypto firms, and that ended up with deposit flight=C2=A0not=C2=A0be=
cause its depositors didn=E2=80=99t trust it but because its depositors wer=
e crypto firms, and=C2=A0their=C2=A0depositors didn=E2=80=99t trust=C2=A0th=
em=C2=A0and withdrew their money.) It can hold Treasuries to maturity, but=
=C2=A0only if there are not too many bank runs.=C2=A0

 =20
   =20
      CS CDS II
   =20
 =20

Sure  why not:

The Credit Derivatives Determinations Committee received a new request to r=
ule on whether Credit Suisse Group AG=E2=80=99s credit default swaps can be=
 triggered after UBS Group AG=E2=80=99s takeover.
The panel was asked whether a bankruptcy credit event had occurred with reg=
ards to the Swiss lender in March, according to a statement on its website.=
 The CDDC had determined on Wednesday that another type of credit event =E2=
=80=94 a government intervention =E2=80=94 hasn=E2=80=99t occurred, which m=
eant that the swaps weren=E2=80=99t triggered.

Did Credit Suisse go bankrupt? No; it agreed to be acquired by UBS in a vol=
untary-ish merger at the strong encouragement of Swiss banking regulators. =
Did it go bankrupt-ish? Ehh, kind of?=C2=A0Its debt was not impaired, but i=
ts additional tier 1 capital securities, which were intended to be written =
down to zero if Credit Suisse got too close to needing a government bailout=
, were written down to zero. This did not affect the Credit Suisse bonds th=
at are subject to CDS, but the game here is, you buy the CDS on the unimpai=
red bonds, and then you argue that something impairment-ish happened in the=
 general neighborhood of those bonds. We have  talked before about argument=
s that the AT1s were =E2=80=9Cnot subordinated=E2=80=9D to the bonds, and t=
hat therefore the write-down of the AT1s should be treated as a write-down =
of the bonds (which were not written down); this argument  did not work. No=
w there is an argument that the write-down of the AT1s should be treated as=
 a bankruptcy and thus trigger CDS on the bonds (which were not written dow=
n). Will this work? I mean, probably not, but it=E2=80=99s worth asking the=
 question.

 =20
   =20
      How fraud works
   =20
 =20

Well,   yes:

A California mutual fund manager was sentenced to four years in prison for =
defrauding investors whose $106 million he said would be put in US Treasury=
 bills and other safe assets but was instead lost in much riskier investmen=
ts. ...
=E2=80=9CMy terrible failure does not represent who I am,=E2=80=9D Abarbane=
l told US District Judge Louis Kaplan, becoming visibly emotional at times.=
 =E2=80=9CI never intended to make them lose money, only to make money.=E2=
=80=9D

It is rare to do fraud with the specific purpose of=C2=A0making your invest=
ors lose money, though I suppose it has happened. Usually the purpose is in=
stead to=C2=A0make money for yourself.=C2=A0The latter often causes the for=
mer, however.

   =20
      Fraud is fun
   =20
 =20

Here is a  US federal criminal case against  a teen named Joseph Garrison, =
who is charged with hacking thousands of accounts on the sports betting sit=
e DraftKings and draining $600,000 from them to his own bank account. =E2=
=80=9CDon=E2=80=99t put it in email,=E2=80=9D I say a lot around here, or i=
n text messages or other electronic chats. But I understand that that is ha=
rd advice to take, particularly for the people doing the crimes.=C2=A0I=C2=
=A0understand that if you are a teen on the internet doing hacking, and you=
 get $600,000 from your hacking, it is=C2=A0not reasonable for me to=C2=A0e=
xpect you=C2=A0not to brag about it to your internet teen friends. It=E2=80=
=99s just that you=E2=80=99ll get caught=C2=A0and this won=E2=80=99t help:
On GARRISON=E2=80=99s cellphone, law enforcement also located conversations=
 between GARRISON and his co-conspirators, which included discussions about=
 how to hack the Betting Website and how to profit from the hack of the Bet=
ting Website by extracting funds from the Victim Accounts directly or by se=
lling access to the Victim Accounts. =C2=A0In one particular conversation, =
GARRISON discussed, in substance and in part, how successful he was at cred=
ential stuffing attacks, how much he enjoyed credential stuffing attacks, a=
nd how GARRISON believed that law enforcement would not catch or prosecute =
him. =C2=A0Specifically, GARRISON messaged the following, in substance and =
in part: =E2=80=9Cfraud is fun . . . im addicted to see money in my account=
 . . . im like obsessed with bypassing [stuff].=E2=80=9D
Weirdly the unnamed conspirator  replied saying =E2=80=9Cidk it ruined my l=
ife personally,=E2=80=9D which, one, yes, and two, why are you still in thi=
s chat? Anyway if you do financial crimes and text your buddies about them =
and say =E2=80=9Cfraud is fun=E2=80=9D and then you get arrested, (1) that=
=E2=80=99s definitely gonna be in the Justice Department press release and =
(2) it is also definitely gonna be in Money Stuff.

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong> <em>Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Monday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Tether reserves</h2>
</td>
</tr>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is a view=C2=A0that crypto was a low-int=
erest-rates phenomenon, but stablecoins are=C2=A0<em>really=C2=A0</em>a low=
-interest-rates phenomenon. The idea of the most popular (=E2=80=9Cfully ba=
cked,=E2=80=9D non-algorithmic) stablecoins is:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You give some company $1.</li>=09<li =
style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It promises to give you back the $1 when you =
want it.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-colo=
r: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">That promise=C2=A0<em>lives on th=
e blockchain;=C2=A0</em>it can be traded on the blockchain, and it represen=
ts $1 on the blockchain.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This description is very, very close to the st=
andard story of=C2=A0<em>banking:=C2=A0</em>You put one paper dollar in the=
 bank, you get back a receipt saying you=E2=80=99ve got $1 in the bank, and=
 you can use that receipt for $1 in the bank to transact with because it=C2=
=A0<em>is a dollar</em>. Your money in your bank account is not a=C2=A0<em>=
claim=C2=A0</em>on money, not an IOU for money; your money in your bank acc=
ount=C2=A0<em>is money</em>. A stablecoin=C2=A0<em>is money</em>, in the sp=
ecific context of some blockchain.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">However, two other things that bank accounts h=
ave are:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Branches, tellers, customer service, =
compliance,=C2=A0stuff like that; and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5p=
x;">
<em>Interest=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0not all of the time, and not so much =
over the past decade, but in an environment of high interest rates it becom=
es reasonable to expect to get paid interest on at least some of the money =
you keep in the bank.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Stablecoins can be like three guys in an undis=
closed location who provide good customer service to a dozen big counterpar=
ties and have an inscrutable website for everyone else. And they tend not t=
o pay interest.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This was a minor omission when banks also tend=
ed not to pay interest, but as rates have gone up, stablecoins seem like an=
 incredibly good business to be in? You have a giant pile of money, you can=
 park it somewhere quite safe and earn like 5% interest, you pay $0 of that=
 interest to your depositors, you pay not very much more than $0 of that in=
terest to your compliance department, you do not have much in the way of op=
erating costs, and you collect 5% of a giant pile of money basically for ha=
ving come up with this idea a few years ago. Of course the downside is that=
 if crypto was a low-interest-rates phenomenon eventually people might=C2=
=A0lose interest in crypto and the demand for stablecoins would dry up. But=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31525316.263428/a=
HR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy90ZXRoZXItdG8tYnV5LW1vcmUtYml0Y29pbi1m=
b3Itc3RhYmxlY29pbi1yZXNlcnZlcy1lYWVlZWUzZD9tb2Q9aXRwX3dzaiZydT15YWhvbw/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd4d54e18" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Tether is fine I guess</a>?</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Crypto company Tether pledged to buy more bitc=
oin for its stablecoin reserves, adding to the $1.5 billion of bitcoin alre=
ady backing its dollar-pegged token.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The company said starting this month it planne=
d to use up to 15% of its net operating profits to purchase bitcoin. It did=
n=E2=80=99t indicate how long or consistently it planned to make the purcha=
ses. Rising interest rates boosted Tether=E2=80=99s profits to $1.48 billio=
n in the first quarter. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tether said it plans for its bitcoin investmen=
ts to be part of the company=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cexcess reserves,=E2=80=9D w=
hich is what it calls additional reserves beyond the value of its liabiliti=
es=E2=80=94the market cap of the tether stablecoin. On May 16, Tether=E2=80=
=99s excess reserves amounted to over $2.4 billion, according to its websit=
e. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Last week the stablecoin issuer reported that =
it held more than $69 billion in cash and cash equivalents at the end of Ma=
rch, including $53 billion invested in U.S. Treasury bills, $7.5 billion in=
 overnight reverse-repurchase agreements and $7.5 billion in money-market f=
unds. The crypto company had billions of dollars in precious metals, secure=
d loans and other investments that include digital tokens.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tether=E2=80=99s latest <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31525316.263428/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuY3Rm=
YXNzZXRzLm5ldC92eXNlODhjZ3dmYmwvMjRHNER1UTBIRTdoN0VRRTZ2R3k0Si84YThhMTcwZWR=
mNjg3ZWEwN2IzZjg2MDQ4YWY4Yjg3Yi9FU08uMDMuMDFfU3RkX0lTQUVfMzAwMFJfT3Bpbmlvbl=
8zMS0wMy0yMDIzX0JET19UZXRoZXJfQ1JSLnBkZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB598e34b1"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;"> reserves report</a> shows <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/31525316.263428/aHR0cHM6Ly90ZXRoZXIudG8vZW4vdGV0aGVycy1sY=
XRlc3QtcTEtMjAyMy1hc3N1cmFuY2UtcmVwb3J0LXNob3dzLXJlc2VydmVzLXN1cnBsdXMtYXQt=
YWxsLXRpbWUtaGlnaC1vZi0yNDRiLXVwLTE0OGItaW4tbmV0LXByb2ZpdC1uZXctY2F0ZWdvcml=
lcy1mb3ItYWRkaXRpb25hbC10cmFuc3BhcmVuY3ktcmV2ZWFscy1iaXRjb2luLWFuZC1nb2xkLW=

oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> roughly $79.4 =
billion</a> of Tether stablecoins outstanding, backed by =E2=80=9Cat least=
=E2=80=9D $81.8 billion of assets, for an excess of $2.4 billion, or, rough=
ly speaking, a capital ratio of about 3%. When we <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31525316.263428/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=
nLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDYtMjkvY3J5cHRvLWxvdmVzLWl0cy1zaGFkb3=
ctYmFua3M_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDUxODIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc=
291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNTE4JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe3bbff4f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  talked about Tether a year ag=
o</a>, that ratio was 0.2%. A 3% capital ratio for a bank with mostly very =
safe short-term assets like Treasury bills would be pretty normal<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>; a 0.2% capital ratio for any sort of=C2=A0bank=C2=A0at all is hair-ra=
ising. Tether has gone from alarmingly thinly capitalized to reasonably wel=
l capitalized over the past year, because it is a pile of money invested in=
 short-term money-market instruments and rates have gone up a lot. Now Teth=
er earns a lot of interest =E2=80=94 it earned more than half of its excess=
 reserves=C2=A0<em>last quarter=C2=A0=E2=80=94 </em>and it puts that intere=
st in the bank, and that means it has more than enough money to pay back al=
l the Tethers out there.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Well, no, I mean, it doesn=E2=80=99t put the i=
nterest in the=C2=A0<em>bank</em>; it puts it in=C2=A0<em>Bitcoin</em>. (Or=
 it puts 15% of it in Bitcoin.) Still, <em>currently,=C2=A0</em>that is all=
 house money:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CEvery single token in the=
 market is and would remain fully backed even if the bitcoin price were to =
go down to zero tomorrow,=E2=80=9D [Tether Chief Technology Officer Paolo]=
=C2=A0Ardoino said in an email. =E2=80=9CTether could distribute the entire=
 amount invested in bitcoin to its shareholders, and the peg to USD will no=
t be affected. In such a scenario, Tether would still have $1B of excess re=
serves.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The ideal way to run Tether, for the people ru=
nning Tether, would be:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Have like $80 billion of Tethers outst=
anding.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Put $80 billion into the s=
afest possible stuff, short-term risk-free instruments, Treasury bills and =
reverse repos with strong counterparties collateralized by Treasuries.</li>=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Earn like $4 billion of interest on th=
at stuff??</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Put $1 billion of the i=
nterest into more safe stuff, just to be safe.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-b=
ottom: 5px;">Put $1 billion of the interest into crazy stuff, to try to gro=
w your assets and make more profits.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px=
;">Pay yourself $2 billion of bonuses, you=E2=80=99ve earned it.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">People are very suspicious of Tether, for basi=
cally good reasons:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It does a long-running comedy routine=
 where it constantly promises to provide audited financial statements any d=
ay now, and never does.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; =
background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It has a history of saying that e=
ach Tether is fully backed by safe US dollar assets, while in fact some of =
them were sometimes backed by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/31525316.263428/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2F=
ydGljbGVzLzIwMTktMDQtMjYvdGhpbmdzLWdvdC13ZWlyZC1mb3Itc3RhYmxlY29pbi10ZXRoZX=
I_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDUxODIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlP=
W5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNTE4JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07c=
eef4b8524a319a23cBef574350" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" =
target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important=
; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-sty=
le: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  dodgy loans to Tether=E2=80=99s affil=
iates</a>.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It seems clear that som=
e Tethers have been created,=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>by customers depositing=
 $1 and getting back one Tether, but by customers depositing $2 worth of Bi=
tcoin (or less?) and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cli=
ck/31525316.263428/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVz=
LzIwMjItMTItMDEvc2JmLW1pc3NlZC1mdHgtcy1yaXNrcz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNTE4MjNfTU9ORVl=
TVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMz=
A1MTgmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB41e1a8e2" it=
emprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"col=
or: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bo=
rder-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #=
ccc;">  getting back one Tether as a loan</a>. This is sort of fractional r=
eserve banking for crypto, and it=C2=A0means that if the price of Bitcoin d=
rops and the borrower can=E2=80=99t pay back the loan, Tether could be part=
ially unbacked.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The counterargument is that this is such a goo=
d and easy business it would be silly=C2=A0for Tether to take any risk: If =
you can earn more than a billion dollars of interest=C2=A0per quarter<em>=
=C2=A0</em>on safe assets with no real expenses, why do anything else?=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose part of the answer is that, if you r=
un Tether, you do not want this business to go away. If you are the bank of=
 crypto, the main risks to your business are:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">People do not trust your reserves, so=
 they take their money out, or</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Peo=
ple do not trust=C2=A0<em>crypto</em>, so they don=E2=80=99t need any Tethe=
rs, so they take their money out.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you=E2=80=99ve got some spare cash to buy B=
itcoin and support the system, that might be a good use of it.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a pretty standard story that people us=
ed to tell about Uber Technologies Inc., back when it was a tech unicorn,=
=C2=A0before it went public:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Uber sells you a nicer car ride than =
a taxi, at a lower price.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">How does=
 it manage this? Well, by losing money on every ride.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">How does it manage=C2=A0<em>that</em>? Well, it raises =
billions of dollars from venture capitalists, which it uses to subsidize=C2=
=A0the losses.=C2=A0</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">How does it m=
anage=C2=A0<em>that</em>? Well, it tells the venture capitalists a good sto=
ry. In the story it tells the VCs, Uber grows its market share rapidly by o=
ffering the best product at the lowest price. It builds network effects: Ev=
ery rider=C2=A0wants to use Uber because it has the best deals, which means=
 every driver wants to drive for Uber because it has all the passengers, wh=
ich means that every rider=C2=A0<em>really=C2=A0</em>wants to use Uber beca=
use all the drivers are there, etc. The network effects make this something=
 of a winner-take-all business, and if Uber wins then it will get all the d=
rivers and all the passengers; it will be the app that people use whenever =
they need a ride. They will stop hailing cabs on the street, or telephoning=
 car services. They will also stop taking buses or subways or personal cars=
; Uber will become just the way to travel. Bus routes will close, subways w=
ill wither, cab drivers will become Uber drivers. Uber=E2=80=99s total addr=
essable market is large, and its market share will grow. Venture capitalist=
s love growing market shares and large total addressable markets.</li>=09<l=
i style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">And, look, when Uber is 99% of the transpor=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But they came pretty close in the blitzscaling=

 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">Venture Predation</a>,=E2=80=9D by Matthew Wansley and Samuel=
 Weinstein:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Predatory pricing is a strategy firms use to s=
uppress competition. The predator prices below its own costs to force its r=
ivals out of the market. After they exit, the predator raises its prices to=
 supracompetitive levels and recoups the cost of predation. The Supreme Cou=
rt has described predatory pricing as =E2=80=9Crarely tried=E2=80=9D and =
=E2=80=9Crarely successful=E2=80=9D and has established a liability standar=
d that is nearly impossible for plaintiffs to satisfy. We argue that one ki=
nd of company thinks predatory pricing is worth trying and at least potenti=
ally successful=E2=80=94venture-backed startups.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A venture predator is a startup that uses vent=
ure finance to price below its costs, chase its rivals out of the market, a=
nd grab market share. Venture capitalists (VCs) are motivated to fund preda=
tion=E2=80=94and startup founders are motivated to execute it=E2=80=94becau=
se it can fuel rapid, exponential growth. Critically, for VCs and founders,=
 a predator does not need to recoup its losses for the strategy to succeed.=
 The VCs and founders just need to create the impression that recoupment is=
 possible, so they can sell their shares at an attractive price to later in=
vestors who anticipate years of monopoly pricing. In this Article, we argue=
 that venture predation can harm consumers, distort market incentives, and =
misallocate capital away from genuine innovations. We consider reforms to a=
ntitrust law and securities regulation to deter it.</p>
</blockquote>
